opp.name: Chemours [Intake/SRM/CLM]
opp.next_step: Need: Timothy expresses a clear need to understand how new tools, especially those leveraging AI, can benefit his role as a category manager and the procurement process within his organization. He highlights struggles with: Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts. "Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P. Supplier risk and compliance issues (environmental testing, sustainability). Integration challenges with Ariba, especially with SAP S/4HANA migration halting previous progress. Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jagger instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics). Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards. Frustration with constant reinvention and lack of a uniform platform due to spinning off legacy SAP applications. He sees AI as a way to "catch up and also leverage it to be more efficient." Timeline: Timothy estimates a formal project may be formed around 2026 .
opp.ais_why:
• Chemours category manager Timothy openly said he wants to understand how AI tools can help his role — a direct, customer-voice AI inquiry.
• Timothy framed AI as a way to catch up and work more efficiently, tying the interest to ongoing Ariba/S4HANA migration frustration.
• A formal project is only expected to form around 2026, so the AI conversation is real but the timing is forward; last activity 3/24/26.

sources: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey (buyer): 'poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts', 'tail-spend leakage, poor catalog governance, high off-contract purchases despite Ariba P2P', 'supplier risk and compliance issues', 'integration challenges with Ariba halted by S/4HANA migration', 'using Jagger for RFX, Sirion for CLM, separate databases, poor dashboards', 'constant reinvention from legacy SAP spin-offs', '$80–100M tail spend'
Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Zycus rep positioned $4–8M potential savings (4.5–9% of $80–100M tail spend) via Anna, but Timothy did not validate or commit to that figure
SF NextStep: Timothy's need statement and pain catalog (matches call content)
metrics.narrative: Timothy articulated clear operational pain on the 16 Sep 2025 discovery call but has not quantified financial impact. Confirmed pains: poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend/contracts; tail-spend leakage and high off-contract purchases despite Ariba P2P; supplier risk and compliance gaps (environmental testing, sustainability); Ariba integration challenges halted by S/4HANA migration pause; underutilization of Ariba for RFX (using Jagger instead); separate Sirion CLM system leading to fragmented databases and poor UX/dashboards; constant reinvention from legacy SAP spin-offs. Timothy estimated Chemours has $80–100M in tail spend, and the Zycus rep positioned Anna's 4.5–9% tail-spend savings (which would equate to $4–8M potential savings for Chemours). However, Timothy did not commit to that ROI figure or quantify the cost of the current pain (e.g. maverick-spend leakage rate, duplicate-system overhead, procurement-cycle time cost). The business problem is identified and the pain owner (Timothy, category manager) is named, but a formal, buyer-validated value case with signed-off metrics does not yet exist. Next move: co-build a quantified ROI model with Timothy and the procurement leadership (likely the procurement director or the decision maker once identified) that measures current-state cost (off-contract %, tail-spend leakage $, cycle time, FTE overhead from triple platforms) vs Zycus future-state savings, and secure their written sign-off on the business case before the RFP stage.

champion.narrative: Timothy Duprey is a champion-in-development, not yet a strong champion. He is a Category Manager (Rotating Equipment, E&I, Refractory and Controls) at Chemours, engaged on the 16 Sep 2025 discovery call, and he articulated clear pain, expressed interest in Zycus's AI/platform story (Merlin Intake, Anna, unified P2P vs Ariba/Jagger/Sirion fragmentation), and stated he is 'advocating for more use of tools that leverage AI.' He has access to the procurement pain (he is an end-user stakeholder) and some organizational visibility (he can estimate tail spend, describe the current-state architecture, and reference the CET executive team's project-prioritization process). However, he explicitly lacks authority: 'I'm only a stakeholder, not the project leader, not part of the CET governing project priorities'—he cannot charter the project, control the budget, or drive the decision. His engagement has been passive since the initial call (no follow-up call in 9 months, though last activity 24 Jun 2026 suggests he is still responsive to outreach). He has not introduced Zycus to the EB, DM, or project team, and he has not shared internal Chemours documents, RFP drafts, or competitive shortlist intel. His strength is DEVELOPING: he is learning, willing to advocate, and still engaged, but he has not yet mobilized internal sponsorship or driven the project forward. He is not at risk of turning detractor, but he is also not actively championing—he is waiting for the organization to form the project. Next move: re-engage Timothy to learn project status, secure his explicit advocacy (ask him to introduce Zycus to the EB/project leader and to mention Zycus in internal project-scoping conversations), and co-develop a mutual action plan that gives him talking points and a business-case framework he can use to elevate the project priority internally. Also identify a second champion closer to budget authority (a procurement director or VP) to complement Timothy's end-user advocacy.

sources: Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'we use Ariba mainly at transactional level—POs, invoicing; haven't leveraged it for auctions, RFX, supplier risk/performance; Ariba migration halted by S/4HANA pause'
Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'we use Jagger for RFI/RFQ and data gathering'
Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'we use Sirion for contract management—came over from sales org, separate databases, inconsistent UX, poor dashboards'
Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Zycus rep positioned PwC replacing Jagger+Ariba, Dow consolidating; Timothy: 'great to see how it all integrates'
No peer vendors (Coupa, Ivalua, GEP, etc.) mentioned; no RFP issued yet
competition.narrative: Incumbent competition is confirmed; peer/new-entrant competition is unknown. Timothy stated on the 16 Sep 2025 call that Chemours currently uses three platforms: (1) SAP Ariba for P2P (transactional POs/invoicing, underutilized for RFX/auctions/supplier performance), (2) Jagger for RFI/RFQ and sourcing, and (3) Sirion for contract lifecycle management (CLM). These are the incumbent solutions Zycus would replace or displace. Timothy described significant friction with all three: Ariba integration progress was 'halted by S/4HANA migration pause,' Jagger is used for the RFX workload Ariba should handle, and Sirion (acquired from Chemours' sales org) has 'separate databases, inconsistent user experience, poor dashboards.' The Zycus rep positioned the PwC reference (replaced Jagger and Ariba with Zycus) and the Dow reference (consolidated onto Zycus from fragmented tools), and Timothy reacted positively. However, no formal RFP has been issued, so the competitive shortlist for the future project is unknown—it is possible Chemours will evaluate other unified S2P platforms (e.g. Coupa, Ivalua, GEP) alongside Zycus, or they may attempt to expand/fix Ariba rather than replace it. The wedge is clear: Zycus offers a single unified AI-driven platform (vs Chemours' current triple-platform fragmentation), with Merlin Intake for end-user adoption (a pain point Timothy emphasized—'constant reinvention, management of change') and Anna for tail-spend automation (directly addressing Timothy's $80–100M tail-spend leakage pain). No other peer vendors have been named. Next move: when the project forms and the RFP is issued, map the full competitive shortlist immediately (ask Timothy or the project team who else is being evaluated), run a competitive-positioning workshop with the Zycus team to lock in our differentiation story (AI + unified platform + PwC/Dow proof points), and ensure the RFP criteria favor Zycus's strengths (AI/automation, platform consolidation, MS Teams integration, tail-spend ROI) over Ariba's breadth or a competitor's incumbent advantage.

sources: Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey: 'poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts', 'multiple divisions, inconsistent visibility between direct COGS and indirect expense', 'tail-spend leakage, poor catalog governance, high off-contract purchases despite Ariba P2P', '$80–100M tail spend', 'supplier risk and compliance gaps—environmental testing, sustainability, Ariba integration challenges from S/4HANA pause', 'management-of-change issues—supplier Ariba registration, account turnover impact PO/invoice visibility', 'Ariba underutilized—transactional POs/invoicing only, not auctions/RFX/supplier performance; using Jagger for RFI/RFQ', 'Sirion for CLM—separate databases, inconsistent UX, poor dashboards', 'legacy SAP spin-offs—iterative integration, constant reinvention, lack of holistic platform', 'AI is right on the cusp… how can we catch up and leverage it to be more efficient?'
SF NextStep (mirrors call): Timothy's need statement and pain catalog
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and owned. Timothy Duprey (Category Manager, Rotating Equipment, E&I, Refractory and Controls) articulated specific operational and strategic procurement pain on the 16 Sep 2025 discovery call: (1) poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts—'multiple divisions have varying category understanding, leading to inconsistent visibility between direct COGS and indirect expense categories'; (2) tail-spend leakage, poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases ($80–100M tail spend estimated) despite having Ariba P2P; (3) supplier risk and compliance gaps—environmental testing, sustainability requirements, and Ariba integration challenges (halted by S/4HANA migration pause); (4) management-of-change issues when suppliers must register in Ariba and account handoffs during turnover impact PO/invoice visibility; (5) underutilization of Ariba—limited to transactional POs/invoicing, not leveraged for auctions, RFX, or supplier performance (RFI/RFQ offloaded to Jagger instead); (6) fragmented contract management—using Sirion (acquired from sales org), resulting in separate databases, inconsistent user experience, and poor dashboards; (7) iterative, non-holistic platform adoption from legacy SAP spin-offs, causing 'constant reinvention' and lack of process consistency. Timothy described the pain owner as himself (category manager, end-user stakeholder) and the broader procurement organization, and framed the compelling event as catching up with AI to 'be more efficient' and reduce the reinvention burden. The pain is operational (inefficiency, visibility gaps, fragmentation) and strategic (competitive disadvantage from slow AI adoption). Confirmed pain; no inference required.

sources: No contracting discussion on the 16 Sep 2025 call
Stage: Qualified (pre-commercial)
Timothy mentioned S/4HANA migration as a prior blocker to Ariba progress—suggests ERP integration will be a key paper-process gate when the deal advances
paper_process.narrative: No contracting, legal, or procurement mechanics have been discussed. The deal is in early Qualified stage (discovery), and no proposal, MSA, SOW, or Order Form has been drafted. InfoSec review, ERP integration scoping (Chemours uses SAP and is mid-S/4HANA migration, which Timothy cited as a blocker to prior Ariba progress), and procurement-channel approval (whether this would go through Chemours' standard vendor-onboarding process or a strategic-initiative fast-track) are all unknown. No partner or SI involvement has been mentioned. No contract-expiration forcing function exists (this is a new platform evaluation, not a renewal). The paper process will become visible only after the project is chartered, the EB/DM are mapped, and commercials begin. Next move: once the deal advances to the relevant stakeholder or Negotiation stage, proactively surface the typical enterprise paper-process timeline (InfoSec questionnaire, ERP integration deep-dive, MSA/SOW legal review, often 60–90 days) so the buyer builds that lead time into their go-live plan, and identify early whether Chemours' legal/procurement teams have standard templates or whether Zycus's paper will be the starting point.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. The 16 Sep 2025 discovery call was with Timothy Duprey, a Category Manager (end-user stakeholder, not budget authority). Timothy explicitly stated he is 'only a stakeholder, not the project leader' and is 'not part of the CET governing project priorities'—he is advocating for tools/AI but does not control the budget or the project decision. No EB-role contact (CFO, CPO, VP Procurement, or exec sponsor) appeared on the call, and the opportunity has zero buyer-side contact roles populated in Salesforce. Account-level contacts include Sameer Ralhan (CFO), Sunil Naik (VP Procurement - Global Indirects), and several procurement directors/managers, but none have been linked to this opportunity or engaged on a call. The evidence shows Timothy is a champion-in-development (access to pain, learning mode, willing to advocate) but not the commercial decision maker. The EB is almost certainly at VP Procurement or CFO level (given the $100k deal size and enterprise platform scope), but that person has not yet surfaced. Next move: ask Timothy directly who will approve the budget and own the business case for this project, get an introduction to that person (likely Sunil Naik, VP Procurement - Global Indirects, or the segment CFO), and conduct a separate discovery call with the EB to validate the pain, the ROI model, and the project priority/timing before any RFP is issued.

decision_process.narrative: No formal decision process or buying committee yet exists; the project has not been chartered. Timothy stated on 16 Sep 2025 that 'a formal project may be formed maybe 2026' and that he is 'not the project leader, not part of the CET governing project priorities.' This indicates the decision process is still pre-formation—Chemours is in an exploration/learning phase, not an active evaluation. Once the project is approved, the typical enterprise motion will be: project team formation → RFI/RFP issuance → vendor shortlist → demos/workshops → commercials → EB/CFO approval → InfoSec review → legal/contracting. The approval chain is unknown but likely multi-level given the scope (replacing/augmenting Ariba, Jagger, Sirion across procurement) and the account size (Fortune 500 chemical company). The last activity (24 Jun 2026, 2 days ago) suggests the deal is still live, but no second call, no project-team intro, and no RFP timeline has been set in the 9 months since the discovery call—implying the project priority has not yet been elevated or the budget/resource allocation is pending. The Zycus rep committed on the 16 Sep 2025 call to 'contact Timothy in one month to check project progress,' but there is no evidence that follow-up occurred or that the project advanced. Next move: re-engage Timothy immediately (he is still responsive per the 24 Jun activity date) to learn whether the project has been chartered, who the project leader and executive sponsor are, what the decision process and timeline now look like, and get introduced to the project team and EB. If the project is still on hold, understand the blocking factor (budget, competing priorities, S/4HANA dependency) and position Zycus to help build the business case to accelerate project approval.

sources: Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy's pain catalog (visibility, tail-spend, risk/compliance, RFX capability, CLM UX, AI efficiency)
Avoma 16 Sep 2025, Timothy's positive reaction to Merlin Intake, Anna, unified platform story, PwC/Dow references
SF Products: eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin Intake, ANA (aligns with buyer's stated needs)
No formal RFP, RFI, or BRD document yet shared (Timothy stated project formation is ~2026)
decision_criteria.narrative: Discovery-stage criteria emerging from the 16 Sep 2025 call, but no formal RFP/RFI or written requirements document yet exists. Timothy's articulated needs map to evaluation criteria: (1) end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend/contracts (a unified platform vs the current Ariba/Jagger/Sirion fragmentation), (2) tail-spend management and catalog governance to reduce off-contract purchases and maverick spend, (3) supplier risk and compliance (environmental, sustainability, Ariba integration post-S/4HANA migration), (4) RFX and sourcing capability (currently offloaded to Jagger because Ariba is underutilized), (5) contract lifecycle management with better UX/dashboards than Sirion, (6) AI/automation to 'catch up and be more efficient'—specifically, Timothy showed interest in Merlin Intake (MS Teams chatbot for end-user adoption) and Anna (autonomous tail-spend negotiation). The Zycus rep presented use cases and reference stories (PwC replacing Jagger+Ariba, Dow consolidating onto Zycus, 40–60% cycle-time reduction, 1–2% total-spend savings), and Timothy responded positively ('great to see how it all overlaps and integrates'). However, these are inferred/early-stage criteria; no formal RFP scoring rubric, no weighted decision matrix, no ShoeFit/BRD document has been shared. The formal decision criteria will be set by the project team and the EB/DM once the project is chartered (estimated 'maybe 2026' per Timothy). Next move: when the project team forms, offer to co-author the RFP requirements and evaluation scorecard with Chemours (positioning Zycus's AI, unified-platform, and P2P integration strengths as must-haves), and secure early visibility into the RFP draft so we can shape the criteria before competitors see them.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Hungry. Timothy explicitly framed AI as a strategic need and a way to 'catch up' with procurement innovation. He asked how AI tools 'can benefit my role as a category manager and the procurement process,' cited 'constant reinvention' and inefficiency as pain points AI could solve, and reacted enthusiastically to the Merlin Intake and Anna demos. No AIS score in Salesforce, but the call transcript confirms high AI appetite.

champion_strength.summary: Timothy Duprey is a developing champion—engaged, pain-aware, and willing to advocate, but lacks authority and has not mobilized internal sponsorship or driven project formation in 9 months. He is the only buyer contact on record. No strong champion exists. Champion-building is the immediate priority: activate Timothy by co-developing a mutual action plan and an exec-ready business case he can use internally, and multi-thread to identify and cultivate a second champion closer to budget authority (procurement director or VP).

If project is still on hold, understand the blocking factor (budget, S/4HANA dependency, competing priorities) and position Zycus to help build the business case to accelerate approval.

items.expected_effect: Eliminate single-point-of-failure risk, map EB and DM, build direct relationship with budget authority and project sponsor, and ensure Zycus is positioned at the executive level before RFP. Multi-threading also surfaces competitive intel (who else is being considered, what the RFP timeline is) and accelerates project approval if the EB is engaged and champions the business case internally.

items.action: Co-build a quantified, exec-ready business case with Timothy and the EB that validates tail-spend savings, platform-consolidation ROI, and cycle-time reduction.
items.trigger: Zycus rep positioned $4–8M potential tail-spend savings on 16 Sep 2025 call, but Timothy did not validate or commit to that figure. No formal ROI model or business case exists. Project formation timeline is uncertain ('maybe 2026'), suggesting the CET has not yet approved budget or prioritized the initiative.
items.expected_effect: Create the business case that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET and secure budget approval. A quantified, buyer-validated ROI (tail-spend savings, Ariba/Jagger/Sirion consolidation cost avoidance, 40% sourcing cycle-time reduction per PwC/Dow proof points) accelerates project charter and positions Zycus as the strategic partner, not just a vendor. Also serves as competitive armor—if SAP or another vendor enters, Zycus already owns the buyer's ROI narrative.

items.action: Position Zycus as S/4HANA-independent and offer a phased approach to remove project-approval blockers.
items.trigger: Timothy stated on 16 Sep 2025 that 'Ariba migration progress halted by S/4HANA project pause'—S/4HANA dependency is a known blocker to procurement-platform progress at Chemours. Project timeline uncertainty ('maybe 2026') suggests competing priorities or risk-aversion may be delaying project approval.
items.expected_effect: Remove the S/4HANA blocker by emphasizing Zycus is cloud-native and ERP-agnostic—can deliver value now without waiting for S/4HANA migration, unlocking tail-spend savings and AI efficiency immediately. Offer a phased approach (Phase 1: Merlin Intake + Anna for quick wins on tail spend and adoption; Phase 2: full P2P replacement after S/4HANA stabilizes) to lower perceived project risk/complexity and accelerate CET approval. This also counters the SAP Ariba 'wait for S/4HANA' narrative.

items.action: Add the missing buyer-side contact roles to the opportunity and expand stakeholder coverage beyond Timothy.
items.trigger: 0 buyer-side contact roles on this opp, below the multi-thread bar. Account-level contacts exist (Sunil Naik VP Procurement, Sameer Ralhan CFO, procurement directors/managers) but are not linked to this opp or engaged in the deal.
items.expected_effect: Ensure CRM hygiene reflects the true stakeholder map once EB/DM/project team are identified, improve forecast defensibility by demonstrating multi-threaded engagement, and create a durable relationship network that survives if Timothy's role changes or the project team expands. This is also a prerequisite for scaling account strategy if Chemours becomes a strategic/enterprise account.

items: Last activity 24 Jun 2026 (2 days ago)—buyer still responsive
Only 1 buyer call on record (16 Sep 2025, 9 months ago)
No EB/DM/project-team contacts added in 9 months
Timothy's sentiment on discovery call was positive—expressed interest, willingness to advocate, no objections or concerns raised
confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live (last activity 2 days ago, 24 Jun 2026) but shallow and passive—single-threaded, no follow-up call in 9 months, no project advancement. Timothy remains responsive and interested (positive sentiment on the discovery call, no negative signals), but the organizational momentum has not materialized. Not cooling (no drop-off in sentiment or responsiveness), but also not accelerating. The deal is stuck in early-stage limbo—waiting for Chemours to charter the project and for Zycus to multi-thread and drive momentum.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 16 Sep 2025, in Qualified 9+ months. Close date 16 Sep 2026 is 82 days from today (26 Jun 2026). Timothy stated on the 16 Sep 2025 call that a formal project may be formed 'in 2026'—meaning project kickoff was not imminent then and would be sometime in calendar 2026. Given the large enterprise motion (discovery → demos → RFI/RFP → shortlist → workshops → commercials → negotiation → InfoSec → contracting, typically 12–15 months), and that we are still in Qualified with no formal project structure, no EB/DM mapped, no champion developed, and no RFP/RFQ cycle yet run, completing all remaining gates in 82 days is unrealistic. The deal IS engaged (last activity 24 Jun, 2 days ago) and healthy—Timothy is learning, pain is clear, Zycus is positioned early—but the close date is a placeholder, not a plan. Expected slip: 6–9 months to Q2/Q3 2027 for a realistic close, aligning with a formal project kickoff in mid-2026 and a 12-month cycle.
evidence: Stage: Qualified (early discovery, no formal project yet)
Last activity: 2026-06-24 (2 days ago, live engagement)
Buyer timeline: Timothy stated on 16 Sep 2025 call 'maybe 2026' for formal project formation
No EB/DM identified, no champion developed, no RFP cycle, no commercials, no contract draft
Enterprise motion for this scope (eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin Intake, ANA replacing Ariba/Jagger/Sirion) requires 12–15 months from project kickoff to close
north_star_verdict.headline: Engaged buyer with clear pain and active learning (last activity 2 days ago), but 9 months in Qualified with no formal project, no EB/champion, and no enterprise motion yet. 82-day close timeline unrealistic without imminent RFP kickoff.

best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal (Qualified, 9 months, no project charter yet) with strong pain-to-product fit but critical execution gaps: no EB/DM mapped, single-threaded on a non-decision-maker, no follow-up in 9 months, and project timeline uncertainty ('maybe 2026'). The buyer is engaged (last activity 2 days ago) but passive—waiting for organizational approval, not driving the deal. Risk: project delay or deprioritization (do-nothing) is the primary threat. Three immediate best practices to raise win probability: (1) Re-engage Timothy NOW to learn project status and get introduced to the EB/project leader before competitors enter or the project stalls further. (2) Multi-thread urgently—expand stakeholder coverage beyond Timothy to procurement leadership (VP Procurement, likely Sunil Naik) and the CET/executive sponsor who will charter the project and control the budget. (3) Co-build a quantified, buyer-validated business case (tail-spend savings, platform-consolidation ROI, cycle-time reduction) that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET and accelerate approval, positioning Zycus as S/4HANA-independent (can deliver value now, not waiting for ERP migration) to remove a known blocker.

competitive_position.summary: Incumbent triple-platform competition confirmed (SAP Ariba, Jagger, Sirion); no peer S2P vendors yet named. Ariba is the primary incumbent but underutilized and blocked by S/4HANA migration pause—medium threat (incumbent inertia, but buyer dissatisfaction is high). Jagger is an active incumbent for RFX (medium threat—serves a need Ariba doesn't, but Timothy showed interest in consolidating onto a single platform). Sirion is the CLM incumbent with poor UX/dashboards (low threat—buyer explicitly dissatisfied, ripe for replacement). The single strongest current threat is INERTIA/DO-NOTHING: no formal project chartered in 9 months since discovery, suggesting budget/priority constraints or competing initiatives may delay or block the evaluation. Zycus is well-positioned (early engagement, pain-aligned story, positive buyer reaction, strong references), but the deal has not advanced to a competitive bake-off. When the RFP is issued, expect Ariba (SAP account team will defend), and possibly Coupa, Ivalua, or GEP as peer challengers. Wedge: unified AI-driven platform (Merlin Intake for adoption, Anna for tail-spend ROI) vs fragmented incumbents; PwC/Dow proof points of replacing Ariba/Jagger; timing advantage (Zycus engaged 9 months early, can shape RFP criteria before competitors enter).

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Ariba migration halted by S/4HANA project pause, limiting Ariba use to transactional POs and invoicing. We haven't leveraged it enough into the middle chunk—supplier risk, auctions, RFX space. Integration challenges with Ariba.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the unified AI-driven P2P platform that eliminates the Ariba integration/underutilization pain. Highlight PwC replacing Ariba+Jagger with Zycus and achieving 40–60% cycle-time reduction. Emphasize that Zycus does not require S/4HANA migration to deliver value (cloud-native, ERP-agnostic). When RFP is issued, ensure criteria favor AI/automation, platform consolidation, and tail-spend ROI over Ariba's breadth.

competitors.name: Jagger (JAGGAER)
competitors.quote: We use Jagger for RFI/RFQ and data gathering. We haven't leveraged Ariba for the RFX space.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus eSourcing + AI as a superior, integrated alternative to Jagger's standalone RFX tool. Use the PwC proof point (replaced Jagger and Ariba with Zycus, 65k users, single platform). Emphasize that consolidating Jagger + Ariba + Sirion onto Zycus eliminates the 'separate databases and inconsistent UX' pain Timothy described, and delivers end-to-end visibility and AI-driven sourcing cycle-time reduction (40% per the rep's positioning).

competitors.name: Sirion
competitors.quote: We use Sirion for contract management—came over from a result of our sales organization. Separate databases, inconsistent user experience, poor dashboards.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
competitors.how_we_win: Sirion is the weakest incumbent—buyer explicitly dissatisfied with UX/dashboards and fragmentation. Position Zycus CLM with AI-powered contract risk scoring, metadata extraction, and dynamic risk ratings (as demoed on the 16 Sep call) as a step-change improvement. Highlight integrated CLM within the unified Zycus P2P platform (vs Sirion's standalone silo), delivering the 'holistic picture' and process consistency Timothy said Chemours lacks.

competitors.name: Do-Nothing / Project Delay
competitors.quote: A formal project may be formed maybe 2026. I'm not the project leader, not part of the CET governing project priorities. We're back and looking at the management portal… but a lot of it was halted because of S/4HANA.
competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
competitors.how_we_win: The single highest current threat is that the project never gets chartered or is deprioritized due to budget constraints, S/4HANA dependency, or competing initiatives. Counter this by: (1) re-engaging Timothy immediately to learn whether the project has been approved and get introduced to the EB/project leader; (2) co-building a quantified business case (tail-spend savings $4–8M, cycle-time reduction, platform consolidation cost avoidance) that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET; (3) positioning Zycus as S/4HANA-independent (cloud-native, can deliver value now without waiting for ERP migration); (4) offering a phased approach (e.g. start with Merlin Intake + Anna for quick tail-spend wins, expand to full P2P later) to lower the project's perceived risk/complexity and accelerate approval.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong and well-received. Timothy explicitly stated AI is a strategic priority ('AI is right on the cusp, how can we catch up and leverage it to be more efficient?'), and the Zycus rep positioned Merlin Intake (MS Teams chatbot for end-user adoption) and Anna (autonomous tail-spend negotiation agent) as direct answers to Timothy's pain. Timothy reacted positively to both ('That's pretty powerful', 'Great to see how it all overlaps and integrates'). The AI story is landing as differentiated and relevant. Not under-positioned.

items.quote: Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts. Multiple divisions have varying category understanding, inconsistent visibility. We have separate databases (Ariba, Jagger, Sirion) and poor user experience.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: End-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts (unified platform)

items.quote: Tail-spend leakage, poor catalog governance, high off-contract purchases despite Ariba P2P. $80–100M tail spend. [Zycus rep positioned Anna autonomous negotiation agent: 4.5–9% savings on tail spend, $4–8M potential for Chemours.] That's pretty powerful.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: Tail-spend management and catalog governance to reduce off-contract purchases

items.quote: AI is right on the cusp, finally working its way into corporate America. How are we using it and how are we gonna leverage it? What's the way we can catch up and leverage it to be more efficient? Great to see how it all overlaps and integrates.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: AI and automation to improve efficiency and adoption

items.quote: We haven't leveraged Ariba for auctions, RFX space—we use Jagger for RFI/RFQ and data gathering. Supplier risk and performance management has been a challenge, analytics is still out there, least respected and understood.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: RFX and sourcing capability (replacing Jagger, compensating for underutilized Ariba)

items.quote: We use Sirion for contract management—came over from sales org, separate databases, inconsistent user experience, poor dashboards. You don't get an effective dashboard at a glance with Sirion like you show here.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: Contract lifecycle management with better UX and dashboards (replacing Sirion)

items.quote: Legacy SAP spin-offs, iterative integration, constant reinvention, lack of holistic platform. It's frustrating—we're not on a uniform platform. Management of change and reeducation—change happens fast but we need consistency in our processes.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy Duprey
items.criterion: Platform consolidation and process consistency (vs legacy SAP spin-off fragmentation)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong product-to-pain fit confirmed on discovery call. Timothy's articulated needs (end-to-end visibility, tail-spend management, AI efficiency, unified platform vs Ariba/Jagger/Sirion fragmentation, better CLM UX) align tightly with Zycus's positioned capabilities (Merlin Intake, Anna, unified S2P, AI-driven analytics, contract risk scoring). Timothy reacted positively to the demo and references ('great to see how it all overlaps and integrates'). No misalignment surfaced. However, formal RFP/BRD criteria do not yet exist, so this is a preliminary fit assessment, not a validated, scored evaluation.

hard.ais_why:
• Chemours category manager Timothy openly said he wants to understand how AI tools can help his role — a direct, customer-voice AI inquiry.
• Timothy framed AI as a way to catch up and work more efficiently, tying the interest to ongoing Ariba/S4HANA migration frustration.
• A formal project is only expected to form around 2026, so the AI conversation is real but the timing is forward; last activity 3/24/26.
hard.next_step:
Need: Timothy expresses a clear need to understand how new tools, especially those leveraging AI, can benefit his role as a category manager and the procurement process within his organization. He highlights struggles with:

  • Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts.
  • "Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P.
  • Supplier risk and compliance issues (environmental testing, sustainability).
  • Integration challenges with Ariba, especially with SAP S/4HANA migration halting previous progress.
  • Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jagger instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics).
  • Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards.
  • Frustration with constant reinvention and lack of a uniform platform due to spinning off legacy SAP applications. He sees AI as a way to "catch up and also leverage it to be more efficient."

Timeline: Timothy estimates a formal project may be formed around 2026.

pulse.summary: Live: last verified Salesforce activity 2 day(s) ago (2026-06-24).

rep_outreach.note: rep reached out, awaiting buyer reply

deltas.subject: Single-threaded on a non-decision-maker: Only contact is Timothy Duprey (Category Manager, end-user stakeholder), who explicitly stated he is 'not the project leader, not part of the CET governing priorities.' No EB, DM, or project-team contacts engaged in 9 months. This is a critical single-point-of-failure risk—if Timothy's advocacy stalls or he leaves, the deal dies. Multi-thread immediately: ask Timothy for an introduction to the procurement director or the decision maker who will lead the project, and engage account-level contacts (Sunil Naik, VP Procurement - Global Indirects, or segment CFO) to build a parallel path to the EB and CET.

No follow-up in 9 months since discovery call: Zycus rep committed on 16 Sep 2025 to 'contact Timothy in one month to check project progress,' but no evidence of that follow-up or any subsequent buyer engagement (only 1 call on record). Deal has been in Qualified for 9 months with no stage progression, no RFP, no project charter. Last activity 24 Jun 2026 (2 days ago) shows the buyer is still responsive, but the relationship is passive—Zycus is not driving momentum. Re-engage Timothy immediately with a specific ask: project status update, introduction to EB/project team, mutual action plan to advance the deal. Use the 9-month gap as a reset opportunity—offer to co-build the business case and RFP criteria to accelerate project approval.

Do-nothing / project-delay is the primary threat: Timothy stated 'a formal project may be formed maybe 2026'—implying budget/priority uncertainty. No project charter in 9 months suggests competing initiatives, S/4HANA dependency, or CET deprioritization may block or indefinitely delay the evaluation. Counter this by positioning Zycus as the catalyst: co-author a concise, exec-ready business case (quantified tail-spend savings $4–8M, platform-consolidation cost avoidance, 40% sourcing cycle-time reduction per PwC/Dow proof points) that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET. Emphasize that Zycus is S/4HANA-independent (cloud-native, can start now without waiting for ERP migration, removing a known blocker Timothy cited). Offer a phased approach (Phase 1: Merlin Intake + Anna for quick tail-spend ROI and adoption wins; Phase 2: full P2P replacement) to lower perceived project risk and accelerate approval.

value.how_we_win: The single highest current threat is that the project never gets chartered or is deprioritized due to budget constraints, S/4HANA dependency, or competing initiatives. Counter this by: (1) re-engaging Timothy immediately to learn whether the project has been approved and get introduced to the EB/project leader; (2) co-building a quantified business case (tail-spend savings $4–8M, cycle-time reduction, platform consolidation cost avoidance) that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET; (3) positioning Zycus as S/4HANA-independent (cloud-native, can deliver value now without waiting for ERP migration); (4) offering a phased approach (e.g. start with Merlin Intake + Anna for quick tail-spend wins, expand to full P2P later) to lower the project's perceived risk/complexity and accelerate approval.

packets.subject: Do-Nothing / Project Delay

value.detail: Project formation timeline is uncertain—Timothy estimated 'maybe 2026' on 16 Sep 2025, and no project has been chartered in the 9 months since. Close date 16 Sep 2026 (82 days out) is not credible. Risk: project approval delayed indefinitely due to budget constraints, S/4HANA dependency (Timothy cited S/4HANA migration pause as a prior blocker to Ariba progress), or competing CET priorities.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'a formal project may be formed maybe 2026', 'S/4HANA project pause halted Ariba migration progress'
value.status: Open—project status unknown, no update in 9 months

value.detail: No EB, DM, or CET/executive sponsor identified or engaged. Timothy is not part of the CET and cannot charter the project. Risk: even if Timothy advocates for Zycus, the project may not be approved or may be deprioritized by the CET, or a different executive may champion a competing solution (e.g. expand Ariba vs replace it).
value.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'I'm not part of the CET governing project priorities'

value.detail: Incumbent SAP Ariba advantage—Chemours is mid-S/4HANA migration, and SAP account team may push to expand Ariba vs replace it, positioning Ariba as the 'safe' choice that integrates with S/4HANA. Risk: EB or CET may default to Ariba expansion (lower perceived risk, existing relationship) unless Zycus demonstrates clear ROI and positions itself as S/4HANA-independent.
value.source: Avoma discovery call 16 Sep 2025, Timothy: 'Ariba migration halted by S/4HANA project pause'

value.flag: Single-threaded on a non-decision-maker: Only contact is Timothy Duprey (Category Manager, end-user stakeholder), who explicitly stated he is 'not the project leader, not part of the CET governing priorities.' No EB, DM, or project-team contacts engaged in 9 months. This is a critical single-point-of-failure risk—if Timothy's advocacy stalls or he leaves, the deal dies. Multi-thread immediately: ask Timothy for an introduction to the procurement director or the decision maker who will lead the project, and engage account-level contacts (Sunil Naik, VP Procurement - Global Indirects, or segment CFO) to build a parallel path to the EB and CET.

value.flag: No follow-up in 9 months since discovery call: Zycus rep committed on 16 Sep 2025 to 'contact Timothy in one month to check project progress,' but no evidence of that follow-up or any subsequent buyer engagement (only 1 call on record). Deal has been in Qualified for 9 months with no stage progression, no RFP, no project charter. Last activity 24 Jun 2026 (2 days ago) shows the buyer is still responsive, but the relationship is passive—Zycus is not driving momentum. Re-engage Timothy immediately with a specific ask: project status update, introduction to EB/project team, mutual action plan to advance the deal. Use the 9-month gap as a reset opportunity—offer to co-build the business case and RFP criteria to accelerate project approval.

value.flag: Do-nothing / project-delay is the primary threat: Timothy stated 'a formal project may be formed maybe 2026'—implying budget/priority uncertainty. No project charter in 9 months suggests competing initiatives, S/4HANA dependency, or CET deprioritization may block or indefinitely delay the evaluation. Counter this by positioning Zycus as the catalyst: co-author a concise, exec-ready business case (quantified tail-spend savings $4–8M, platform-consolidation cost avoidance, 40% sourcing cycle-time reduction per PwC/Dow proof points) that Timothy and the EB can use to justify the project to the CET. Emphasize that Zycus is S/4HANA-independent (cloud-native, can start now without waiting for ERP migration, removing a known blocker Timothy cited). Offer a phased approach (Phase 1: Merlin Intake + Anna for quick tail-spend ROI and adoption wins; Phase 2: full P2P replacement) to lower perceived project risk and accelerate approval.

He highlights struggles with:Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts."Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P.Supplier risk and compliance issues (environmental testing, sustainability).Integration challenges with Ariba, especially with SAP S/4HANA migration halting previous progress.Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jagger instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics).Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards.Frustration with constant reinvention and lack of a uniform platform due to spinning off legacy SAP applications. Need: Timothy expresses a clear need to understand how new tools, especially those leveraging AI, can benefit his role as a category manager and the procurement process within his organization. He highlights struggles with:

  • Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts.
  • "Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P.
  • Supplier risk and compliance issues (environmental testing, sustainability).
  • Integration challenges with Ariba, especially with SAP S/4HANA migration halting previous progress.
  • Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jagger instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics).
  • Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards.
  • Frustration with constant reinvention and lack of a uniform platform due to spinning off legacy SAP applications. He sees AI as a way to "catch up and also leverage it to be more efficient."

Timeline: Timothy estimates a formal project may be formed around 2026.\xa0

' (offending: 'Need: Timothy expresses a clear need to understand how new tools, especially those leveraging AI, can benefit his role as a category manager and the procurement process within his organization. He highlights struggles with:Poor end-to-end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts."Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P.Supplier risk and compliance issues (environmental testing, sustainability).Integration challenges with Ariba, especially with SAP S/4HANA migration halting previous progress.Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jagger instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics).Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards.Frustration with constant reinvention and lack of a uniform platform due to spinning off legacy SAP applications. He sees AI as a way to "catch up and also leverage it to be more efficient."Timeline: Timothy estimates a formal project may be formed around 2026.')
